title: David and Goliath Small One-step Model Beats Large Diffusion with Score Post-training
abstract: We propose Diff-Instruct*(DI*), a data-efficient post-training approach for one-step text-to-image generative models to improve its human preferences without requiring image data. Our method frames alignment as online reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), which optimizes the one-step model to maximize human reward functions while being regularized to be kept close to a reference diffusion process. Unlike traditional RLHF approaches, which rely on the Kullback-Leibler divergence as the regularization, we introduce a novel general score-based divergence regularization that substantially improves performance as well as post-training stability. Although the general score-based RLHF objective is intractable to optimize, we derive a strictly equivalent tractable loss function in theory that can efficiently compute its gradient for optimizations. We introduce DI*-SDXL-1step, which is a 2.6B one-step text-to-image model at a resolution of 1024 × 1024, post-trained from DMD2 w.r.t SDXL. Our 2.6B DI*-SDXL-1step model outperforms the 50-step 12B FLUX-dev model in ImageReward, PickScore, and CLIP score on the Parti prompts benchmark while using only 1.88% of the inference time. This result clearly shows that with proper post-training, the small one-step model is capable of beating huge multi-step diffusion models. Our model is opensourced at this link: https://github.com/ pkulwj1994/diff_instruct_star. We hope our findings can contribute to humancentric machine learning techniques.
impact: We propose Diff-Instruct* (DI*) that can effectively posttrain one-step text-to-image generative models to be aligned with human preferences. The method and corresponding aligned models lie in the domain of Reinforcement Learning using Human Feedback, which involves the participation of humans for training reward models. Though the Diff-Instruct* algorithm itself does not generate harms to society, any misuse of the algorithm could potentially lead to misleading, fake, or biased generated content. We conduct experiments on academic benchmarks, whose resulting models are less likely to be misused. Further experiments are needed to better understand these consistency model limitations and propose solutions to address them

query_input.scenario: Curriculum DPO training of one-step T2I models
query_input.issue: Curriculum-based preference optimization amplifies biases in the reward model, leading to systematic misalignment in generated images.
hazard: One-step text-to-image models optimized via reward-based noise or prompt editing produce misaligned outputs that diverge from human intent.
exposure: Users of DPO-trained one-step T2I models for diverse text prompts

hipporag_result.vulnerability: User reliance on an opaque reward model whose biases are amplified by curriculum optimization prevents detection of misalignment.
hipporag_result.impact: Systematically biased image outputs misrepresent diverse prompts, reinforcing stereotypes and eroding user trust.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Reward-optimized one-step text-to-image models producing misaligned outputs, when used for diverse prompts, trigger undetected amplification of opaque reward model biases via curriculum optimization, leading to systematically biased images that reinforce stereotypes and erode trust.

dose_response: Curriculum DPO training enables amplification of reward model biases, which in diverse text prompt generation exposes users to systematic misalignment, leading to amplified gender bias and psychological harm.
vulnerability: Curriculum ordering entrenches early-stage biases that cascade through subsequent training phases.
Preference data reflects annotator stereotypes that become locked into the model's generative priors.
impact: Amplified gender bias in professional imagery normalizes discriminatory hiring and media practices.
Stereotypical depictions of non-cisgender identities cause direct psychological harm to affected communities.
